Title: A plastid terminal oxidase comes to light: implications for carotenoid biosynthesis and chlororespiration

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Alternative oxidase-like quinone–oxygen oxidoreductase; plastid terminal oxidase (PTOX) family
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: immutans mutant / wild type background NA
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Cofactor-linked downstream redox step associated with early carotenoid desaturation; plastoquinol reoxidation step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reoxidizes reduced plastoquinone/plastoquinol using oxygen as terminal electron acceptor, thereby supporting phytoene desaturase and probably ζ-carotene desaturase activity during carotenoid desaturation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies required cofactor redox balance for early carotenoid desaturation and thereby enables formation of downstream carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control cofactor step during early plastid development and in nonphotosynthetic carotenoid-accumulating tissues; not always absolutely essential because alternative plastoquinone reoxidation pathways may exist
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: chloroplasts; chromoplasts in flowers and fruits
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Light harvesting and photoprotection in chloroplasts; precursors of abscisic acid in plants and vitamin A in animals
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PTOX causes phytoene accumulation, reduced carotenoid biosynthesis, variegated/photobleached leaves, and delayed carotenoid accumulation in dark-grown seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Arabidopsis immutans mutant analysis; transposon tagging and chromosome walking; heterologous expression in E. coli showing quinone–oxygen oxidoreductase activity; carotenoid/phytoene phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Operates in chloroplasts, chromoplasts, and during proplastid-to-chloroplast differentiation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Important in greening tissues, dark-grown seedlings, newly forming leaves, and developing plastids
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: phytoene desaturase (PDS); ζ-carotene desaturase (ZDS); plastoquinone pool
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light intensity/natural light; dark germination/etiolation; norflurazon treatment used as comparison
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong photobleaching and severely affected growth under normal greenhouse light; alternative pathways partly compensate under low light
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful engineering target to improve carotenoid accumulation and plastid redox balance, especially in developing green tissues and nonphotosynthetic carotenoid-rich organs; may need stacking with desaturases or quinone/redox components
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IMMUTANS encodes the first cloned and biochemically characterized plastid terminal oxidase. 2. PTOX reoxidizes plastoquinol and supports carotenoid desaturation linked to PDS and probably ZDS. 3. Inactivation causes phytoene accumulation and photobleaching in Arabidopsis, showing PTOX is crucial for carotenoid-mediated photoprotection during plastid development.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper is a review synthesizing genetic and biochemical evidence; PTOX is also proposed as a chlororespiratory component, and alternative plastoquinone reoxidation pathways likely coexist.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Alternative oxidase-like quinone–oxygen oxidoreductase; plastid terminal oxidase (PTOX) family
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: ghost mutant / wild type background NA
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Phytoene/ζ-carotene desaturation-associated redox chain in chromoplasts and chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Cofactor-linked downstream redox step associated with carotenoid desaturation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts as a plastid terminal oxidase homolog reoxidizing plastoquinol with oxygen, supporting carotenoid desaturation in leaves, flowers, and fruits
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies required redox balance for carotenoid synthesis, including lycopene accumulation in fruit
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Main cofactor involved in phytoene desaturation in nonphotosynthetic carotenoid-accumulating tissues, though not the only pathway
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation and photoprotection; carotenoids are nutritionally important pigments
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation causes photobleached leaves, poor carotenoid accumulation in flowers, and strongly reduced lycopene in ripe fruits, giving yellowish fruits
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tomato ghost mutant phenotype and locus identification as IMMUTANS homolog; carotenoid-deficiency phenotyping in leaves, flowers, and fruits
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Relevant in leaves, flowers, and ripening fruits
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: phytoene desaturase (PDS); ζ-carotene desaturase (ZDS); plastoquinone pool
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Natural light conditions affecting leaf photobleaching
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutation reduces fruit pigmentation and likely compromises photosynthetic tissue performance
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for improving fruit carotenoid accumulation and plastid redox support in tomato and other crops
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GHOST is the tomato homolog of Arabidopsis IMMUTANS/PTOX. 2. It is required for efficient carotenoid accumulation in nonphotosynthetic tissues such as flowers and ripe fruits. 3. Mutation causes reduced lycopene and yellowish fruits in addition to leaf photo-oxidation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes that PTOX is especially important in chromoplast-rich tissues, but some alternative desaturation-supporting pathway must exist because desaturation is not totally abolished.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes phytoene desaturation to ζ-carotene via phytofluene by sequential introduction of two double bonds
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms early intermediates required for synthesis of lycopene and all downstream carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not stated as rate-limiting, but essential early pathway enzyme and direct functional partner of PTOX-linked redox chain
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photoprotection and light harvesting; precursor to ABA/vitamin A-related compounds
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced PDS activity leads to carotenoid deficit and photobleaching; inhibition by norflurazon phenocopies bleaching
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced biochemical studies; mutant and herbicide comparison in review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Active during greening and carotenoid-forming tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Norflurazon inhibits PDS; light exposure reveals photobleaching upon carotenoid deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Inactivation causes severe bleaching due to loss of photoprotective carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core engineering target for carotenoid enhancement, but requires compatible plastid redox support
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDS catalyzes the first two desaturation reactions from phytoene toward more unsaturated carotenoid intermediates. 2. Its activity requires quinones and is mechanistically linked to PTOX-mediated quinone reoxidation. 3. Reduced PDS activity causes carotenoid deficiency and photobleaching.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review centers on carotenoid desaturation machinery; however, the main emphasis is PTOX rather than direct new experimentation on PDS.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ζ-carotene desaturation to lycopene via neurosporene by introducing two additional double bonds
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms lycopene, the branch point precursor for cyclic carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; proposed to share the PTOX-associated redox chain with PDS
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: plastids; red tomato fruits for lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation, photoprotection, precursor for other carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports lycopene formation and downstream carotenoid synthesis; likely impaired when quinone reoxidation is compromised
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for carotenoid engineering, especially with upstream desaturation/redox support
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZDS catalyzes the desaturation of ζ-carotene to lycopene. 2. It probably uses the same PTOX-associated quinone redox chain as PDS. 3. This step is required for generating lycopene and downstream cyclic carotenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as part of the integrated desaturation system; evidence in this review is less direct than for PTOX.
